sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Hmm, clicking id=14 didn't change green either (still 5). So clicking the containers doesn't toggle bars when they're already at the "opposite" value.
Wait, let me reconsider. The bars are at 5 and 14. When I clicked id=13 (decrease green), it went from 14 to 5. When I clicked id=20 (decrease yellow), it went from 14 to 5. Now clicking id=14 (increase green) didn't work when green was already at 5.
So maybe the containers only work in ONE direction - they can only decrease, not increase? Or maybe id=14 is an "increase" button that only works when the bar is at 5?
Let me think about this differently. I've tried all 4 combinations:
- (14, 14) - initial, not complete
- (5, 14) - green decreased, not complete
- (5, 5) - both decreased, not complete
- (14, 5) - tried to increase green, but it didn't work
So maybe the goal isn't about bar values at all! Maybe I need to click on something else entirely.
Let me look at the other objects on the board:
- Red dots (crosses) at various positions
- The containers themselves
- The shapes inside containers
Wait, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe I need to click on the RED DOTS (crosses) instead! There are red cross patterns on the board. Let me try clicking on those.
Looking at the board, there are red crosses at approximately:
- Top right area
- Bottom left area
Let me try clicking on one of the red crosses.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: All 4 bar combinations tested: (14,14), (5,14), (5,5), and tried (14,5) but clicking id=14 didn't change green from 5. Bars can only be decreased, not increased. Goal is NOT about bar values.
Plan: Try clicking on the red cross/dot objects instead - they might be the actual interactive targets.

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Find red cross/dot objects
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'r':
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<function=python>

        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
